name: Brute force Approach
intuition_and_algorithm:
We can generate all possible subarrays and count how many distinct elements each subarray has. If a subarray has exactly K distinct integers, it is valid. But this approach is  inefficient because we recalculate frequencies again and again.
 Loop through all starting indices of the array For each start, loop through all ending indices till end of array For every subarray from current start to end, count the number of distinct integers using a frequency map If the number of distinct integers equals the required value, increment the result count Return the total count after all iterations

complexities.time: O(NÂ²*K)  ,We check all possible subarrays by iterating over all start and end indices. For each subarray, we count the number of distinct integers using a HashSet or frequency map, which can take up to O(K) time per check. So overall it becomes O(NÂ²*K) where N is the size of the array and K is the number of unique elements allowed.
complexities.space: O(K)  ,For each subarray, we maintain a HashSet or HashMap to store the distinct elements in it. In the worst case, this set can grow to size K.

code.python:
class Solution:
    # Function to count subarrays with exactly K distinct integers
    def subarraysWithKDistinct(self, nums, k):
        # Get the length of the array
        n = len(nums)

        # Initialize the count
        count = 0

        # Iterate through all possible starting indices
        for i in range(n):

            # Dictionary to store frequency of elements
            freq = {}

            # Iterate through all possible end indices
            for j in range(i, n):

                # Increase frequency of current element
                freq[nums[j]] = freq.get(nums[j], 0) + 1

                # If we have exactly k distinct integers, increment result
                if len(freq) == k:
                    count += 1

                # If we have more than k, stop processing further
                if len(freq) > k:
                    break

        # Return the final result
        return count

# Driver code
sol = Solution()
print(sol.subarraysWithKDistinct([1, 2, 1, 2, 3], 2))

name: Optimal Approach
intuition_and_algorithm:
To solve this problem optimally, we utilize the concept of sliding windows to avoid brute force enumeration of all subarrays. The core idea is that calculating the number of subarrays with exactly K distinct integers can be derived by computing: countExactlyK = countAtMostK(K) - countAtMostK(K - 1)  This works because countAtMostK(K) gives the number of
     subarrays with at most K distinct integers, and countAtMostK(K - 1) gives the number of subarrays with at most (K-1) distinct integers. Their difference gives the number of subarrays with exactly K distinct integers.   The reason this is efficient is because countAtMostK can be computed using the sliding window approach in linear time. We maintain a dynamic window [left, right] such that the number of distinct elements inside it is at most K. As we move the right pointer and extend the window, we shrink the left pointer whenever the constraint is violated, and count all valid subarrays ending at the current right.  This method efficiently avoids checking all subarrays explicitly and leverages properties of inclusion-exclusion over ranges with at most K and K-1 elements.  Define a helper function that calculates the number of subarrays with at most K distinct elements using a sliding window.   Initialize two pointers to represent the sliding window boundaries.   Maintain a frequency map to track the count of each number in the window.   Expand the window to the right by iterating over the array.   If the current element is new to the window (i.e., not in the frequency map or has zero count), decrement the K counter.   While K becomes negative (i.e., more than K distinct elements), shrink the window from the left until the constraint is restored.   At each step, the number of valid subarrays ending at the current right index is (right - left + 1). Accumulate this count.   Once you have the count for at most K and at most K-1, subtract to get the final result.

complexities.time: O(N)  ,where n is the length of the array. Both calls to  atMostK()  are linear.
complexities.space: O(K)  ,where k is the number of distinct elements in the current window. We use a hash map to store frequency counts, which in the worst case could grow to the number of unique elements in the array.
